content.problemStatement:
It is well known that the routing algorithm used on the Internet is highly non-optimal. A "hop", in Internet jargon, is a pair of nodes that are directly connected - by a cable or a microwave link or whatever. The number of hops that a packet may take in going from one node to another may be far more than the minimum required.

But the routing algorithm used by the Siruseri Network company is worse. Here, a packet sent from one node to another could even go through the same node twice or even traverse the same hop twice before it eventually finds its way to its destination. Sometimes a packet even goes through the destination more than once before it is considered "delivered". Suppose the network in Siruseri consisted of the following nodes and cables:



There are 555 nodes and 888 cable links. Note that a pair of nodes may be connected by more than one link. These are considered to be different hops. All links are bidirectional. A packet from node 111 to node 555 may, for example, travel as follows: 111 to 222, 222 to 111, 111 to 333, 333 to 222, 222 to 111, 111 to 444, 444 to 555, 555 to 444, 444 to 555. This routing is of length 999 (the number of hops is the length of a given routing). We are interested in counting the number of different routings from a given source to a target that are of a given length.

For example, the number of routings from 111 to 222 of length 333 are 777. They are as follows (separated by ;): 111 to 222, 222 to 111 and 111 to 222; 111 to 333, 333 to 111 and 111 to 222; 111 to 444, 444 to 111 and 111 to 222; 111 to 555, 555 to 111 and 111 to 222; 111 to 444, 444 to 333 (via the left cable) and 333 to 222; 111 to 444, 444 to 333 (via the right cable) and 333 to 222; 111 to 222, 222 to 333 and 333 to 222.

You will be given a description of the network at Siruseri as well as a source, a target and the number of hops, and your task is to determine the number of routings from the source to the target which have the given number of hops. The answer is to be reported modulo 423734237342373.

Input:The first line of the input consists of a single integer NNN indicating the number of nodes in the Siruseri. This is followed by NNN lines each containing NNN positive integers. The ithi^{th}ith integer on line jjj is the number of links connecting node iii with node jjj. (The ithi^{th}ith entry on line jjj will be the same as the jthj^{th}jth entry on line iii. Further, the ithi^{th}ith entry on line iii will always be 000. ) This is followed by a line with 333 integers S,TS, TS,T and KKK, the source, the target and the number of hops.

Output:A single integer indicating the number of routings from SSS to TTT with KKK hops modulo 423734237342373.
constraints: S and T are distinct nodes
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the network. This is followed by N lines, each containing N positive integers, representing the number of links between each pair of nodes. The next line contains three integers S, T, and K, representing the source node, target node, and the number of hops, respectively.
content.outputFormat: A single integer indicating the number of routings from S to T with K hops modulo 423734237342373.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are 7 different routings from node 1 to node 3 with 3 hops.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to compute the number of routings from the source node to all other nodes for each possible number of hops. We will use a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the number of routings from the source node to node j with i hops. We will iterate over all nodes and all possible number of hops, and for each node, we will iterate over all its neighbors and update the dp array accordingly. Finally, we will return the value of dp[K][T] modulo 423734237342373.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 * K) — because we are iterating over all nodes, all possible number of hops, and all neighbors of each node.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * K) — because we need to store the dp array of size N * K.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys
MOD = 423734237342373
input = sys.stdin.readline
N = int(input())
adj = [list(map(int, input().split())) for _ in range(N)]
S, T, K = map(int, input().split())
dp = [[0] * (K + 1) for _ in range(N)]
dp[S - 1][0] = 1
for i in range(1, K + 1):
    for j in range(N):
        for k in range(N):
            if adj[j][k] > 0:
                dp[k][i] = (dp[k][i] + dp[j][i - 1] * adj[j][k]) % MOD
print(dp[T - 1][K])
